text: Santo Domingo, founded in 1498, is the oldest continuously inhabited European-established city in the Americas; its Colonial Zone is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
The Catedral Primada de America in Santo Domingo's Colonial Zone, begun in 1514, is the oldest cathedral in the Americas.
The Ozama Fortress in Santo Domingo, built 1502-1508, is the oldest standing European military structure in the Americas.
The first university in the Americas, the Universidad Santo Tomas de Aquino (today's UASD), was founded in Santo Domingo in 1538.
Merengue, the Dominican Republic's national music and dance, was inscribed on UNESCO's Intangible Cultural Heritage list in 2016.
Bachata, born in Dominican barrios in the early 1960s, joined merengue on UNESCO's Intangible Cultural Heritage list in 2019.
Pico Duarte, at about 3,098 meters, is the highest mountain in the entire Caribbean - and it occasionally gets frost.
Lake Enriquillo is the Caribbean's largest lake and sits roughly 40 meters below sea level, the lowest point on any Caribbean island - and it's home to American crocodiles.
The Dominican flag is the only national flag in the world that features an image of the Bible.
Dominican independence was declared on February 27, 1844, led by Juan Pablo Duarte and the secret society La Trinitaria - which is why February 27 is celebrated with Carnival's biggest parades.

The Mirabal sisters, Dominican activists assassinated in 1960, are honored worldwide: the UN chose November 25, the date of their death, as the International Day for the Elimination of Violence against Women.
The Dominican Republic produces more Major League Baseball players than any country outside the United States.
Larimar, a sky-blue gemstone, is found in only one place on Earth: the Barahona province of the Dominican Republic.
The Dominican Republic is one of the few sources of blue amber in the world, mined in the mountains near Santiago.
Every winter, thousands of humpback whales migrate to Samana Bay to mate and calve, making it one of the best whale-watching spots in the world (roughly January to March).
Everyday English words like 'hurricane' (huracan), 'hammock' (hamaca), 'barbecue' (barbacoa), and 'canoe' (canoa) come from the Taino language of Hispaniola's first inhabitants.
Hispaniola, shared by the Dominican Republic and Haiti, is one of only two Caribbean islands split between two countries - the other is tiny Saint Martin.

La bandera ('the flag') - rice, stewed beans, meat, and salad - is the Dominican Republic's everyday national dish, while sancocho, a hearty stew that can mix up to seven meats, headlines celebrations.
